attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic at eight weeks gestation with her first pregnancy. She reports frequent tension headaches but is worried about taking any medications during pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her booking blood tests and dating scan are normal.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Neurological examination is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate advice regarding paracetamol use in pregnancy?
options: Use paracetamol only in the second and third trimesters
Use paracetamol only if prescribed by a doctor
Avoid all paracetamol use throughout pregnancy
Switch to ibuprofen instead of paracetamol
Regular paracetamol use is safe throughout pregnancy
attributes.explanation:
This scenario addresses the common concern about medication safety in pregnancy. The Australian categorisation system for medications in pregnancy provides guidance for healthcare providers and patients about medication safety.

Regular paracetamol use is considered safe throughout pregnancy and is classified as Category A. This means it has been taken by a large number of pregnant women without any proven increase in the frequency of malformations or other harmful effects on the foetus.

Avoiding all paracetamol is unnecessarily restrictive and may lead to poor pain control and increased maternal stress. There is no evidence to support complete avoidance.

Using paracetamol only in the second and third trimesters is also unnecessarily restrictive as it is safe throughout all trimesters of pregnancy.

Using paracetamol only if prescribed is overly cautious as it is available over-the-counter and has a well-established safety profile in pregnancy.

Switching to ibuprofen would not be appropriate as non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are Category C medications and should be avoided in pregnancy, particularly in the third trimester due to risks including premature closure of the ductus arteriosus.

When counselling pregnant women about medication use, it is important to balance the benefits of treating maternal symptoms against any potential risks to the foetus. In this case, paracetamol provides a safe option for managing headaches during pregnancy.
learningPoints: Paracetamol is classified as Category A in pregnancy and is considered safe throughout all trimesters.
The Australian categorisation system for medications in pregnancy ranges from A (safest) to X (contraindicated).
Patient education about medication safety in pregnancy is an important component of antenatal care.
references: Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA). Australian categorisation system for prescribing medicines in pregnancy [Internet]. Canberra: TGA; [cited 2024 Nov 22]. Available from: https://www.tga.gov.au/australian-categorisation-system-prescribing-medicines-pregnancy
